title: Sensor Setup: Building the DShield Honeypot and SIEM
body: This first entry doesn’t cover a single attack — it documents the build-out of the honeypot sensor and SIEM that everything else on this page is generated from.

The sensor is a Raspberry Pi 5 running SANS ISC’s DShield sensor software on my home network, with Cowrie emulating SSH/Telnet and isc_agent emulating a vulnerable web server. A self-hosted DShield-SIEM stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana, Filebeat in Docker) gives me a local copy of the data independent of what gets reported back to SANS ISC.

Getting traffic to actually reach the sensor took longer than expected — UniFi’s zone-based firewall treats NAT port forwarding and firewall permission as two separate layers, so both a port forward and an explicit Allow rule were needed for every exposed port. A second round of networking trouble came from getting the Pi’s DMZ zone talking to the SIEM VM’s isolated KVM network on my own PC — resolved with a firewalld port forward plus a matching UniFi rule, and a libvirt-zone-specific allow rule once that turned out to be a second, separate reject layer.
Full write-up, including the Logstash ILM pipeline failure and the disk-retention setup using a UniFi NAS for nightly snapshots, is in Attack Observation #1.
